questions.question: Why is it generally considered unethical to plagiarize someone else's work?

options.A: It is only unethical if the original author loses money.
options.B: It takes unearned credit for another person's effort and undermines trust.
options.C: It is a victimless act as long as the work is of high quality.
options.D: It is ethical because information should be free.

questions.question: What is the primary ethical argument for preserving the environment?

options.A: It prevents nature from taking over human cities.
options.B: It sustains the ecosystems necessary for the well-being of current and future generations.
options.C: It ensures that corporations can continuously exploit natural resources forever.
options.D: It strictly serves to make natural landscapes visually appealing for tourism.

questions.question: Why is nepotism generally frowned upon in a professional workplace?

options.A: It bypasses meritocracy and can lead to unqualified individuals holding power.
options.B: It forces families to spend too much time together.
options.C: It always results in the immediate bankruptcy of the company.
options.D: It guarantees that the business will be successful because family members trust each other.

questions.question: What makes a formal apology effective in conflict resolution?

options.A: Explaining in detail why the other person is actually at fault.
options.B: Minimizing the event so the victim stops being upset.
options.C: Taking genuine accountability for the harm caused without making excuses.
options.D: Only apologizing if the other person promises to forget the event entirely.

questions.question: What is the primary social function of politeness?

options.A: It is a method of establishing dominance over weaker individuals.
options.B: It acts as social lubrication to reduce interpersonal friction and show respect.
options.C: It is designed to waste time before getting to the point.
options.D: It strictly exists to hide a person's true malicious intentions.

questions.question: What is the most defensible reason to tip a server in a country where tipping is the norm?

options.A: It proves to the server that you are wealthier than they are.
options.B: It gives you the right to treat the server poorly during the meal.
options.C: It adheres to the social contract in a system where their base wage assumes tips.
options.D: It ensures the food will be cooked better next time.

questions.question: Why is gossiping generally viewed as a negative social behavior?

options.A: It is an ineffective way to communicate basic, objective facts.
options.B: It usually results in the gossiper losing their voice.
options.C: It can spread misinformation and damage reputations without giving the subject a chance to defend themselves.
options.D: It prevents people from forming any social bonds.

questions.question: What makes constructive criticism different from an insult?

options.A: Constructive criticism aims to help the recipient improve, whereas an insult intends to demean them.
options.B: Constructive criticism must always be delivered in writing.
options.C: Insults are strictly factual, while criticism is always subjective.
options.D: Constructive criticism only points out things the person already knows.

questions.question: Why is volunteer work generally considered a virtuous activity?

options.A: It is the fastest way to become financially wealthy.
options.B: It demonstrates altruism by sacrificing personal time for the benefit of others.
options.C: It proves that the volunteer is morally superior to everyone else.
options.D: It is a legal requirement for citizenship in most countries.

questions.question: Why do societies generally agree that children should not be held to adult legal standards?

options.A: Children lack the fully developed cognitive reasoning to fully understand the consequences of their actions.
options.B: Children are inherently malicious and cannot be reasoned with.
options.C: Adults simply do not care what children do.
options.D: Children are physically unable to commit significant crimes.

questions.question: Why is it ethically problematic to use a smartphone while driving?

options.A: It drains the phone's battery too quickly.
options.B: It distracts the driver, unreasonably increasing the risk of fatal accidents to innocent bystanders.
options.C: It prevents the driver from listening to the car radio.
options.D: It makes the car engine run less efficiently.

questions.question: What is the primary societal value of studying history?

options.A: It allows people to memorize dates to win trivia games.
options.B: It ensures that old technologies are always used instead of new ones.
options.C: It provides context for current events and helps societies avoid repeating past mistakes.
options.D: It proves that humanity has never made any real progress.

questions.question: Why is "gaslighting" considered psychological abuse?

options.A: It involves physically turning off the lights in someone's home to scare them.
options.B: It is a healthy tactic used to encourage critical thinking in a partner.
options.C: It intentionally manipulates someone into doubting their own sanity, memory, or perception of reality.
options.D: It simply means disagreeing with someone's opinion during a debate.

questions.question: What is the moral argument for treating animals humanely?

options.A: Animals are sentient beings capable of experiencing pain and suffering, which should be minimized.
options.B: Animals will eventually evolve to punish humans if we do not.
options.C: Treating them well ensures they taste better.
options.D: Animals are biologically identical to human beings.

questions.question: What makes a joke cross the line into bullying?

options.A: When the "joke" repeatedly targets a person's vulnerabilities and causes them distress.
options.B: When the joke does not make everyone in the room laugh out loud.
options.C: When the joke contains a pun.
options.D: Bullying only occurs if physical violence is explicitly threatened.

questions.question: In the context of a debate, why are "ad hominem" attacks considered bad practice?

options.A: They are too polite and fail to prove a strong point.
options.B: They attack the person's character rather than addressing the substance of their argument.
options.C: They require too much factual evidence to execute properly.
options.D: They strictly attack the moderator rather than the opponent.

questions.question: What is the primary ethical danger of algorithmic bias in artificial intelligence?

options.A: It makes the AI operate too slowly to be useful.
options.B: It causes the AI to become self-aware and hostile to humans.
options.C: It can automate and scale historical discrimination, leading to unfair treatment of marginalized groups.
options.D: It forces the AI to only output responses in a single language.

questions.question: Why is empathy generally considered essential for good leadership?

options.A: It allows a leader to understand their team's needs, build trust, and make decisions that consider human impact.
options.B: It makes the leader weak and easy to overthrow.
options.C: It guarantees that the leader will agree to every request made by an employee.
options.D: It replaces the need for any technical competence.

questions.question: What is the most justifiable reason for a society to maintain a criminal justice system?

options.A: To provide jobs for lawyers and judges.
options.B: To protect the public, deter harmful acts, and provide a structured means of rehabilitation and restitution.
options.C: To legally oppress the poorer classes of citizens.
options.D: To create entertaining true-crime documentaries.

questions.question: What is the ethical flaw in the "might makes right" philosophy?

options.A: It assumes that physically strong people are always highly intelligent.
options.B: It equates moral justification with power, abandoning justice and protecting the strong at the expense of the weak.
options.C: It requires too much exercise to maintain.
options.D: It prevents military conflicts from ever occurring.

questions.question: Why is "ghosting" widely viewed as a harmful way to end a relationship?

options.A: It denies the other person closure and leaves them confused and doubting themselves.
options.B: It is legally actionable in most jurisdictions.
options.C: It forces the person being ghosted to move to a new city.
options.D: It means the relationship never actually happened.

questions.question: What is a universally accepted reason why corruption in government is harmful?

options.A: It makes government processes move too quickly.
options.B: It diverts public resources for private gain and destroys the public's trust in institutional fairness.
options.C: It forces politicians to be too honest with their constituents.
options.D: It causes taxes to automatically drop to zero.

questions.question: Why is the scientific method generally trusted over anecdotal evidence?

options.A: It relies on older, ancient texts.
options.B: It is controlled entirely by a single global corporation.
options.C: It relies on systematic observation, controlled testing, and peer review rather than subjective, isolated experiences.
options.D: Anecdotal evidence is always a deliberate lie.

questions.question: What is the arguable moral foundation of "fair use" in copyright law?

options.A: It allows people to steal anything as long as they don't sell it.
options.B: It protects large corporations from paying independent artists.
options.C: It balances the creator's right to profit with the public's right to engage with, critique, and build upon cultural works.
options.D: It proves that copyright law is entirely useless.

questions.question: What is the subjective but widely accepted benefit of creating art?

options.A: It guarantees the artist will become immortal.
options.B: It provides a medium for human expression, cultural reflection, and emotional processing.
options.C: It is the only scientifically proven way to cure physical illnesses.
options.D: It forces people to stop talking.

questions.question: Why is it generally considered important to keep an open mind during a disagreement?

options.A: You might be wrong, and being open allows for the possibility of learning and reaching a more accurate conclusion.
options.B: It guarantees that you will win the argument eventually.
options.C: It confuses your opponent into giving up.
options.D: It allows you to ignore the other person completely.

questions.question: What is the primary ethical argument against monopolies in a free-market economy?

options.A: They make products too cheap for consumers to value them.
options.B: They cause the stock market to immediately crash.
options.C: They stifle innovation, reduce consumer choice, and allow a single entity to unfairly control prices.
options.D: They result in too many different types of the exact same product.

questions.question: What makes compromise a necessary part of a functioning democracy?

options.A: Because citizens have competing, valid interests, finding middle ground is required to govern without authoritarianism.
options.B: It ensures that nobody gets what they want, making everyone equally miserable.
options.C: It is a sign of moral weakness and political failure.
options.D: It allows the loudest citizens to always win.

questions.question: Why is micromanagement generally viewed negatively by employees?

options.A: It gives employees too much free time.
options.B: It signals a lack of trust, stifles autonomy, and often reduces overall productivity and morale.
options.C: It means the manager is doing all the physical labor.
options.D: It causes the company's servers to crash.

questions.question: What is the broadly accepted danger of living entirely within an ideological "echo chamber"?

options.A: It causes literal damage to a person's hearing over time.
options.B: It forces a person to change their mind every day.
options.C: It reinforces existing biases, prevents intellectual growth, and fosters extreme polarization.
options.D: It makes a person entirely immune to propaganda.

questions.question: Why is cheating on a standardized test generally considered wrong?

options.A: It invalidates the purpose of the assessment and unfairly disadvantages honest test-takers.
options.B: It makes the test too easy for the teachers to grade.
options.C: It causes the physical test paper to degrade.
options.D: It means the student will literally forget everything they learned.

questions.question: What is a common ethical justification for whistleblower protection laws?

options.A: Employees should be encouraged to invent lies about their bosses for financial gain.
options.B: Employees should be able to expose dangerous corporate practices without fear of retaliation, protecting the public.
options.C: Whistleblowers are always morally flawless individuals.
options.D: It forces companies to publicly display all of their trade secrets.

questions.question: Why is holding a grudge often said to be more harmful to the person holding it?

options.A: It maintains a state of internal bitterness that prevents personal healing and emotional forward-movement.
options.B: It physically curses the person they are holding the grudge against.
options.C: It causes immediate amnesia.
options.D: It makes the person unable to feel physical pain.

questions.question: Why do societies generally value the concept of a "fair trial"?

options.A: To ensure justice is based on evidence and objective law, protecting individuals from the tyranny of the mob.
options.B: To give criminals a chance to escape through legal loopholes.
options.C: To make sure the trial takes as long as possible.
options.D: To provide television networks with dramatic broadcasting material.

questions.question: What makes "toxic positivity" unhelpful to someone experiencing grief?

options.A: It makes the grieving person laugh too much.
options.B: It invalidates their genuine pain and pressures them to suppress negative emotions rather than processing them.
options.C: It is highly radioactive.
options.D: It forces the grieving person to forget who they lost entirely.

questions.question: Why is "greenwashing" considered a deceptive business practice?

options.A: It forces the company to paint all of its products the color green.
options.B: It makes the products legally immune to taxation.
options.C: It misleads consumers into believing a company is environmentally friendly, diverting support from truly sustainable practices.
options.D: It actively destroys the ozone layer directly through advertising.

questions.question: What is the primary ethical issue with "deepfake" technology when used without consent?

options.A: It makes movies too cheap to produce.
options.B: It violates a person's digital consent and can be used to destroy reputations or spread disinformation.
options.C: It enhances the acting skills of the person being faked.
options.D: It strictly causes computer viruses.

questions.question: Why is punctuality generally respected in professional cultures?

options.A: It proves that a person has no personal life.
options.B: It is the only way to prove you own a watch.
options.C: It demonstrates reliability and shows respect for the time and schedules of colleagues.
options.D: It ensures that meetings will finish early.

questions.question: Why is self-awareness considered a critical component of emotional intelligence?

options.A: It allows you to perfectly predict the future.
options.B: Recognizing your own emotional triggers is the first step to managing your reactions and interacting fairly with others.
options.C: It means you never have to sleep.
options.D: It gives you the ability to read other people's minds precisely.

questions.question: What makes the concept of "planned obsolescence" ethically questionable in manufacturing?

options.A: It deliberately designs products to fail, forcing consumers to spend more money and creating environmental waste.
options.B: It makes products last infinitely long, bankrupting the company.
options.C: It ensures that products are always of the highest possible quality.
options.D: It makes products aesthetically ugly on purpose.

questions.question: Why do people generally argue that true courage requires the presence of fear?

options.A: Fear gives humans superhuman physical strength.
options.B: If there is no fear or risk, an action requires no moral or physical fortitude to perform.
options.C: Courage is simply a biological mutation.
options.D: People who do not feel fear are legally classified as cowards.

questions.question: What is the widely held moral view on the difference between a mistake and a calculated betrayal?

options.A: Mistakes are legally punishable, while betrayals are not.
options.B: Betrayals can only happen between strangers.
options.C: A mistake implies a lack of malicious intent, whereas a calculated betrayal requires a conscious decision to deceive.
options.D: Mistakes are always made on purpose but hidden well.

questions.question: Why is diversity often argued to be beneficial in decision-making groups?

options.A: It brings a wider range of perspectives, which helps prevent blind spots and groupthink.
options.B: It ensures that decisions take years to be finalized.
options.C: It strictly fulfills legal quotas without any practical benefit.
options.D: It guarantees that everyone will always agree immediately.

questions.question: What is the underlying ethical principle of the "Golden Rule" (treat others as you want to be treated)?

options.A: It promotes fundamental empathy, asking individuals to recognize the shared humanity in others.
options.B: It requires everyone to buy each other gold jewelry.
options.C: It assumes everyone has the exact same hobbies and preferences.
options.D: It encourages people to ignore others completely.

questions.question: Why is financial literacy generally considered empowering?

options.A: It allows individuals to legally print their own money.
options.B: It guarantees that a person will become a billionaire overnight.
options.C: It gives individuals the knowledge to make informed decisions, avoid predatory debt, and secure independence.
options.D: It makes a person immune to paying taxes.

questions.question: What makes the "slippery slope" argument a logical fallacy in many debates?

options.A: It assumes that a relatively small first step will inevitably lead to an extreme and disastrous chain of events without evidence.
options.B: It implies that ice is dangerous to walk on.
options.C: It relies entirely on mathematical formulas to win an argument.
options.D: It demands immediate physical action from the opponent.

questions.question: Why is it generally considered poor social etiquette to check your phone repeatedly during a one-on-one conversation?

options.A: Phones emit radiation that harms the other person.
options.B: It signals that whatever is on your screen takes priority over the person in front of you, making them feel devalued.
options.C: It is only rude if the other person is your employer.
options.D: Checking your phone legally voids any agreement made during the conversation.

questions.question: What is the primary reason that communities often treat "outsiders" with more suspicion than their behavior warrants?

options.A: Outsiders are statistically more likely to be criminals.
options.B: In-group familiarity creates a baseline trust that newcomers haven't yet earned, which can harden into unfair bias.
options.C: Outsiders always dress in visually threatening ways.
options.D: Most communities have legal charters barring entry to strangers.

questions.question: Why is publicly praising one employee's work in a way that implicitly demeans others generally considered a poor management practice?

options.A: Praise is legally restricted to private settings in most labor agreements.
options.B: It motivates the praised employee so intensely they become unmanageable.
options.C: It creates a competitive, zero-sum atmosphere that undermines team cohesion and can humiliate those not mentioned.
options.D: Other employees will immediately quit upon hearing a colleague complimented.

questions.question: Why is it considered socially insensitive to immediately try to "fix" a problem when a friend comes to you expressing emotional distress?

options.A: Offering solutions is illegal without a therapist's license.
options.B: It redirects the conversation away from their need to feel heard and validated, which is often what they're actually seeking.
options.C: Solutions are always technically wrong in emotional contexts.
options.D: It makes the friend feel too happy too quickly.

questions.question: What is the most widely accepted reason why social norms around mourning (e.g., how long grief should last) can be harmful?

options.A: Mourning periods are legally fixed by government statute.
options.B: Grief follows a universal biological timeline that societies always accurately reflect.
options.C: They impose an external deadline on an internal emotional process, making people feel abnormal for grieving at their own pace.
options.D: Mourning rituals are only harmful in cultures that don't practice them.

questions.question: Why is repeating a rumor while framing it as "just asking questions" generally considered a form of dishonesty?

options.A: Questions are legally binding statements in most jurisdictions.
options.B: It allows the speaker to spread damaging implications while maintaining plausible deniability for the harm caused.
options.C: It confuses the listener because questions should never contain information.
options.D: Rumors become true once phrased as a question.

questions.question: What makes "love bombing" — overwhelming a new romantic partner with excessive affection early on — a recognized red flag in relationships?

options.A: It is medically proven to cause heart disease.
options.B: Excessive early affection is always a sign of true love and should be embraced.
options.C: It can be a manipulation tactic that creates dependency and bypasses the natural, gradual process of building genuine trust.
options.D: It is only a red flag if the gifts are inexpensive.

questions.question: Why do people often find it uncomfortable when someone accepts a compliment with no deflection whatsoever?

options.A: Direct acceptance of praise violates most cultural norms of modesty, which expect at least token humility even if the compliment is deserved.
options.B: Compliments are only valid if the recipient disagrees with them.
options.C: People who accept compliments are statistically more aggressive.
options.D: A compliment ceases to be true the moment the recipient agrees with it.

questions.question: What is the primary ethical concern with framing a group's struggle in terms of what a more powerful group "allows" them to do?

options.A: It is grammatically incorrect in formal writing.
options.B: It subtly reinforces the legitimacy of the more powerful group's authority to grant or withhold rights in the first place.
options.C: It causes the more powerful group to feel guilty, which is always counterproductive.
options.D: Such framing makes legal change impossible in all recorded history.

questions.question: Why is it generally considered unfair to judge a person's capability based on how confidently they present themselves?

options.A: Confidence and competence are perfectly correlated in all professional domains.
options.B: Quiet or uncertain presentation styles are legally protected traits.
options.C: Confidence is partly a product of privilege and socialization, so equating it with ability disadvantages people who were never taught to self-promote.
options.D: Confident people always perform worse under pressure.

questions.question: Why is "code-switching" — adjusting your language and behavior depending on the social setting — often described as an unfair burden placed on marginalized groups?

options.A: It is a medically exhausting condition with no social dimension.
options.B: It requires marginalized individuals to constantly suppress their authentic identity to meet the comfort standards of a dominant culture.
options.C: Only politicians are required to code-switch, so it is a professional hazard, not a social one.
options.D: Code-switching improves cognitive function and is therefore always beneficial.

questions.question: Why is the "bystander effect" considered a failure of social responsibility rather than simply rational self-interest?

options.A: Bystanders are legally prohibited from intervening in most public situations.
options.B: Because diffused responsibility leads each individual to assume someone else will act, resulting in collective inaction that harms the victim.
options.C: Intervening always makes the situation physically worse, so inaction is optimal.
options.D: The bystander effect only occurs when the bystanders personally know the victim.

questions.question: What is the primary social critique of "performative activism" — publicly supporting a cause without taking substantive action?

options.A: It is illegal to support causes you have not personally experienced.
options.B: Public displays of support generate too much attention for the cause and distract from its goals.
options.C: It prioritizes the appearance of moral virtue for the activist over meaningful impact for those actually affected.
options.D: Performative activism always converts into genuine activism within six months.

questions.question: What makes "weaponized incompetence" — deliberately performing a task poorly to avoid being asked again — a form of social manipulation?

options.A: It is only manipulative if the person is paid for the task.
options.B: It shifts an unfair share of labor onto others by exploiting their lower tolerance for substandard work.
options.C: Incompetence is a protected characteristic and cannot be weaponized.
options.D: It motivates the other party to develop better skills themselves.

questions.question: Why is "tone policing" — criticizing how someone expresses their grievance rather than addressing the grievance itself — considered a derailing tactic?

options.A: Emotional expression is governed by strict legal standards of acceptability.
options.B: It shifts the conversation from the validity of the complaint to the manner of its delivery, allowing the underlying issue to go unaddressed.
options.C: Tone policing always results in a more productive conversation.
options.D: It is only a derailing tactic when used in written communication.

questions.question: What is the core ethical problem with "gatekeeping" — policing who is or isn't a legitimate member of a subculture or interest group?

options.A: It is a violation of international copyright law.
options.B: Subcultures have a legal obligation to remain open to new members at all times.
options.C: It uses arbitrary criteria to exclude people, prioritizing the gatekeeper's ego over the shared enjoyment that communities are meant to provide.
options.D: Gatekeeping reliably improves the quality of all subcultures it is applied to.

questions.question: Why do psychologists argue that "moral licensing" — feeling entitled to behave badly after doing something good — is a genuine risk to ethical behavior?

options.A: Good deeds physically drain a person's capacity for further good deeds.
options.B: A single virtuous act creates a psychological sense of "credit" that can unconsciously justify subsequent selfish or harmful behavior.
options.C: Moral licensing only applies to religious communities where good deeds are formally tracked.
options.D: Doing good deeds is statistically linked to criminality.

questions.question: Why is the "just-world hypothesis" — the belief that people always get what they deserve — considered a cognitive bias rather than an accurate model of reality?

options.A: It is a purely mathematical theory with no social applications.
options.B: It leads people to blame victims for their misfortune, as this preserves a comforting but inaccurate belief that the world is fundamentally fair.
options.C: The world has been statistically proven to be perfectly just.
options.D: It only applies to people who have never experienced hardship.

questions.question: What is the main social critique of "respectability politics" — the idea that marginalized groups should conform to dominant cultural norms to earn equal treatment?

options.A: It is only critiqued in academic settings and has no practical relevance.
options.B: It places the burden of ending discrimination on its victims rather than on those perpetuating it.
options.C: Conforming to dominant norms has been proven to be completely ineffective at improving social outcomes.
options.D: It is a legal strategy that requires no critique because it is codified in civil rights law.

questions.question: Why is giving an unsolicited gift with clear strings attached generally considered a form of social coercion rather than generosity?

options.A: Gifts are legally void unless given freely.
options.B: It uses the social obligation created by a gift to manufacture leverage over the recipient's future behavior.
options.C: Gifts with conditions are universally accepted as the standard form of gift-giving.
options.D: The recipient is always legally required to fulfill the attached condition.

questions.question: What is the primary reason "social loafing" — exerting less effort in a group than alone — is considered a structural problem rather than purely a character flaw?

options.A: Group members are biologically weaker than individuals.
options.B: Reduced individual accountability in groups creates conditions where reduced effort is the rational response, regardless of personal work ethic.
options.C: Social loafing only occurs in groups larger than 100 people.
options.D: It is a character flaw and has no structural dimension.

questions.question: Why do social psychologists argue that "the halo effect" — assuming someone is good in all ways because they excel in one — is a problematic basis for judgment?

options.A: It exclusively benefits unattractive people.
options.B: One positive trait is legally sufficient evidence of overall character.
options.C: It causes us to overlook real flaws and grant unearned trust based on a single, often superficial, characteristic.
options.D: The halo effect has been experimentally disproven and no longer applies.

questions.question: Why is "virtue signaling" criticized even when the cause being supported is genuinely worthwhile?

options.A: Supporting good causes is always ethically suspect.
options.B: When the primary motivation appears to be self-presentation rather than the cause itself, it risks reducing serious issues to social currency.
options.C: Virtue signaling is illegal in most democracies.
options.D: Publicly supporting a cause automatically delegitimizes it.

questions.question: What makes double standards in gender expectations particularly difficult to challenge socially?

options.A: They are encoded in the legal systems of every country without exception.
options.B: Double standards by definition cannot be observed by the people who hold them.
options.C: They are so deeply embedded in cultural norms that violating them is penalized even by people who consciously disagree with them.
options.D: Gender expectations are biologically fixed and therefore immune to social change.

questions.question: Why is excessive flattery often perceived as less trustworthy than measured, specific praise?

options.A: Flattery is legally defined as fraud in professional settings.
options.B: When praise is indiscriminate and disproportionate, it suggests the flatterer's goal is influence rather than honest feedback, undermining their credibility.
options.C: People are psychologically incapable of believing positive things about themselves.
options.D: Specific praise is always factually incorrect.

questions.question: Why do sociologists argue that "social capital" — the value of one's personal and professional networks — can perpetuate inequality?

options.A: Social networks are legally restricted to people above a certain income bracket.
options.B: People with more social capital are biologically more intelligent.
options.C: Access to valuable networks is often inherited or shaped by privilege, meaning those already advantaged gain further advantages through connections alone.
options.D: Social capital has no measurable impact on economic outcomes.

questions.question: What makes it socially fraught to point out that someone's story is factually incorrect in a group setting?

options.A: Correcting someone in public is a criminal offense under slander law.
options.B: It forces the person to choose between accepting the correction and suffering public embarrassment, which can feel like an attack on their credibility regardless of intent.
options.C: Facts are entirely subjective, making any correction inherently invalid.
options.D: Publicly incorrect stories become legally true once three people have heard them.

questions.question: Why is "pity" generally considered a less constructive response to someone's hardship than empathy?

options.A: Pity is a medically recognized condition that harms the person who feels it.
options.B: Pity positions the sufferer as inferior and the observer as fortunate, whereas empathy seeks to understand the experience as an equal.
options.C: Empathy and pity are functionally identical and the distinction is purely semantic.
options.D: Pity is the legally mandated response to hardship in most jurisdictions.

questions.question: Why is the norm of "reciprocal self-disclosure" — matching the level of personal openness another person shows you — important in building trust?

options.A: Sharing equal amounts of information is required by law in formal relationships.
options.B: It signals mutual investment and creates a balanced vulnerability that allows both parties to feel safe opening up further.
options.C: It ensures that neither person ever has to share anything truly private.
options.D: People who disclose more than their partner are considered more trustworthy.

questions.question: What is the central tension in the social norm that people should "earn" respect rather than receive it by default?

options.A: Respect cannot be earned through any observable behavior.
options.B: It legally conflicts with constitutional protections in most countries.
options.C: While earned respect may feel more meaningful, treating people with baseline dignity regardless of status is also a broadly held value — meaning the two norms frequently conflict.
options.D: The norm has been universally abandoned in modern societies.

questions.question: Why is it generally considered poor social etiquette to give unsolicited advice?

options.A: It is illegal in most jurisdictions without a professional license.
options.B: It implies the recipient is incapable of managing their own life and hasn't asked for your input.
options.C: Advice is only valid when delivered in written form.
options.D: People are biologically unable to process information they didn't request.

questions.question: What is the most widely accepted reason that social hierarchies in workplaces can become harmful?

options.A: They prevent the CEO from interacting with employees.
options.B: They are universally illegal under labor law.
options.C: When power differentials suppress dissent, errors go unchallenged and individuals feel unable to contribute honestly.
options.D: Hierarchies cause employees to forget how to do their jobs.

questions.question: Why is "saving face" — allowing someone to avoid public embarrassment — often valued in social interaction?

options.A: It preserves a person's dignity and makes continued cooperation more likely.
options.B: It is only practiced in East Asian cultures and has no broader relevance.
options.C: It ensures the embarrassed person will owe you a favor.
options.D: Public embarrassment is medically dangerous.

questions.question: What is the primary reason that oversharing personal information early in a relationship can feel uncomfortable to the recipient?

options.A: Most people are legally prohibited from hearing private information.
options.B: It creates an asymmetry of vulnerability that the other person hasn't consented to and may feel burdened by.
options.C: Personal information is inherently boring to others.
options.D: It means the person has no filter, making them physically dangerous.

questions.question: Why do people often feel social discomfort when accepting a favor they cannot immediately reciprocate?

options.A: Most people instinctively prefer to be in debt to others.
options.B: Favors are legally binding contracts in most cultures.
options.C: Accepting without reciprocating can feel like it disrupts the implicit balance of mutual exchange that underlies many relationships.
options.D: The recipient is physically allergic to generosity.

questions.question: What makes "negging" — giving backhanded compliments to lower someone's self-esteem — an ethically problematic social tactic?

options.A: It is only problematic if the target is a minor.
options.B: It is a compliment, so it cannot cause harm by definition.
options.C: It deliberately undermines a person's confidence to gain psychological leverage over them.
options.D: It lowers the social status of the person giving the compliment.

questions.question: Why is the expectation that employees remain perpetually cheerful at work often critiqued?

options.A: Cheerfulness makes coworkers uncomfortable.
options.B: It is a form of emotional labor that can suppress authentic expression and create undue psychological strain.
options.C: Happiness has been shown to reduce productivity by 90%.
options.D: Most employment contracts legally prohibit positive emotions.

questions.question: What is the most defensible reason that "venting" to a friend repeatedly about the same problem can strain the friendship?

options.A: Friends are legally required to offer only one session of emotional support.
options.B: It signals to the friend that their time and emotional investment are not valued if no effort is made to address the problem.
options.C: Negative emotions are physically contagious and toxic to the listener.
options.D: Friends become bored very quickly and move on after two conversations.

questions.question: Why do societies generally view the silent treatment as a passive-aggressive rather than healthy form of conflict avoidance?

options.A: It is only passive-aggressive when used by men.
options.B: Silence is legally classified as verbal abuse in most countries.
options.C: It withholds communication as a form of punishment, preventing resolution while still asserting emotional dominance.
options.D: It is too noisy to be effective.

questions.question: What is the core tension that makes "radical honesty" — saying everything you think without filter — controversial as a social value?

options.A: Most people are unable to physically hear complete honesty.
options.B: Honesty is only appropriate when delivered in a formal setting.
options.C: While truth-telling is generally valued, unfiltered honesty can disregard the harm words cause, prioritizing the speaker's comfort over the listener's well-being.
options.D: Radical honesty guarantees that all relationships will improve permanently.
